rationale.AWR: CAPS multidisciplinary detection plus systemwide UC student-wellbeing initiatives give multiple channels, but counselor ratios run above recommended and contingent/intl coverage is uneven. Signal: CAPS + UC mental-health programs.
rationale.EMP: Title IX Civil Rights Office and confidential resources exist, but 2024 encampment response (delayed protection during counter-protester attack, then mass arrest) showed processing over felt care. Signal: encampment handling.
rationale.ACT: Triage and emergency resources exist; high demand and waitlists at a ~46k-student campus keep responsiveness mid-range. Signal: CAPS capacity vs enrollment.
rationale.EQU: Strong on UC access mission (large Pell share, completion-gap tracking) though race-conscious admissions banned by Prop 209; relatively high equity floor. Signal: UC IPEDS access data.
rationale.BND: UAW-won ASE contract (post-2022 strike, large raises) materially improved grad-worker conditions via third-party leverage, not institutional initiative. Signal: UAW BX contract.
rationale.ACC: Encampment policing widely condemned by faculty/departments; accountability for the April 30 security failure was limited and contested. Signal: department condemnation statements.
rationale.SYS: UC system advocates on access/financial-aid policy and sustained mental-health investment, but structural critique is constrained by state politics. Signal: UC systemwide advocacy.
rationale.INT: Values-behavior gap exposed in 2024: stated safety commitments vs slow response to violence against its own students; consistency under pressure was weak. Signal: 2024 protest response.

rationale.AWR: Victim Care Unit, residential care teams, and post-2019 reforms create proactive detection; hostel-security and disclosure changes followed a documented blind-spot finding. Signal: post-Baey reforms.
rationale.EMP: Subsidised specialist counselling for harassment survivors and a dedicated care unit show structural validation, though the original 2019 Dean's response was widely criticised as cold. Signal: Victim Care Unit.
rationale.ACT: Unlimited subsidised counselling for affected students and strengthened reporting pathways indicate proportional response. Signal: subsidised counselling.
rationale.EQU: Strong access via national bursaries and need-based aid; equity framing is more universalist than identity-disaggregated. Signal: MOE/NUS aid.
rationale.BND: Student-conduct and consent processes tightened after Baey case; PhD/staff labor conditions less publicly documented. Signal: hostel safety reforms.
rationale.ACC: Notable transparency: NUS publishes half-yearly sexual-misconduct reports disclosing complaint counts including staff — rare self-disclosure. Signal: half-yearly misconduct reports.
rationale.SYS: Reforms addressed proximate causes (security, penalties) more than structural drivers; limited public structural advocacy in Singapore context. Signal: reform scope.
rationale.INT: Reforms were reputationally forced by Baey backlash, but the institution did follow through with sustained reporting — mixed but improving. Signal: sustained reporting cadence.

rationale.AWR: Dedicated Harassment Consultation Centers and student counseling exist campus-wide, but power-harassment (pawahara) of grad students is a known under-detected problem. Signal: Harassment Counseling Center.
rationale.EMP: Confidential harassment consultation is structured, yet hierarchical supervisor culture limits felt validation for doctoral students. Signal: consultation infrastructure.
rationale.ACT: Counseling services are available but capacity and proactive outreach are limited relative to demand; English-language outcome data thin. Signal: Student Counseling Center.
rationale.EQU: Persistent gender imbalance (low share of women students/faculty) and limited disaggregated equity reporting; access programs modest. Signal: gender ratio.
rationale.BND: Doctoral labor and supervisor-dependency risks are real in the lab system; consent/workload protections not strongly documented. Signal: lab hierarchy.
rationale.ACC: Harassment cases surface but institutional self-disclosure and reparative practice are limited in public English evidence. Signal: limited disclosure.
rationale.SYS: Long-horizon planning capacity is strong institutionally, but structural critique of the koza/lab system is muted. Signal: governance.
rationale.INT: Established support structures but personality/department-dependent application; evidence thin — scored conservatively. Confidence: medium.

rationale.AWR: Differentiated counseling (TUM4Health, TUM4Mind, Graduate School wellbeing, Diversity office) targets multiple groups including doctoral candidates. Signal: TUM4Health/TUM4Mind.
rationale.EMP: Multiple structured contact points and diversity advising suggest care infrastructure, but felt-experience evidence is thin in English. Signal: counseling spectrum.
rationale.ACT: Psychological counseling plus external-provider partnerships extend capacity; outcome data not public. Signal: TUM4Health psychological counseling.
rationale.EQU: Active diversity/gender-equality apparatus and German tuition-free access aid equity; disaggregated completion data limited publicly. Signal: TUM.Diversity.
rationale.BND: German Wissenschaftszeitvertragsgesetz fixed-term-contract precarity affects early-career researchers sector-wide; TUM not exempt. Signal: fixed-term contract norms.
rationale.ACC: Standard German complaint structures exist; little public evidence of self-disclosed harm or reparative action. Signal: limited disclosure.
rationale.SYS: Strong long-term research strategy and wellbeing institutionalization; structural critique modest. Signal: Graduate School wellbeing program.
rationale.INT: Values infrastructure present and broadly institutionalized; evidence medium — scored conservatively.

rationale.AWR: Counselling and wellbeing services exist with safer-community programs, but the wage-theft scale shows staff distress went undetected/ignored for years. Signal: counselling services.
rationale.EMP: Casual academics describe being processed as cheap labor; wage-theft case undercuts felt care for contingent staff. Signal: NTEU accounts.
rationale.ACT: Student services are functional; remediation of underpayment was reactive and slow per NTEU. Signal: remediation pace.
rationale.EQU: Australian equity-access programs and Indigenous commitments aid equity, but casual-staff exploitation is a major equity failure. Signal: casual pay differential.
rationale.BND: Major boundary failure: ~A$72m wage-theft enforceable undertaking, 31 underpayment types since 2017, casuals most affected — systemic exploitation. Signal: Fair Work undertaking.
rationale.ACC: University acknowledged underpayment and entered enforceable undertaking with contrition payment — accountability, though only after regulator action. Signal: A$72m back-pay pledge.
rationale.SYS: Sector-leading remediation framework could be a model nationally; some structural learning. Signal: remediation programme.
rationale.INT: Stated staff-care values contradicted by years of systematic underpayment; integrity gap is documented and large. Signal: wage-theft Federal Court action.

rationale.AWR: Student counselling and wellbeing services exist, but 42%+ of casual staff struggling to pay bills signals widespread undetected/ignored staff distress. Signal: UCU casualisation data.
rationale.EMP: Mass redundancy plan and disputed 'no deficit' framing during cuts left staff feeling unheard; low felt care amid restructuring. Signal: UCU picket testimony.
rationale.ACT: Services run but capacity strained by cuts; staff requests (no compulsory redundancies) were refused. Signal: cuts dispute.
rationale.EQU: Scottish free-tuition access aids equity, but casualised-staff precarity and proposed 1,800 job losses fall hardest on insecure workers. Signal: casual-contract job losses.
rationale.BND: Boundary failure: high casualisation, hidden redundancies among hourly/fixed-term staff, 51+ hr workweeks — depleting, not sustainable. Signal: workload/casualisation.
rationale.ACC: Industrial dispute is public and ongoing; institution has not substantially conceded, limited acknowledgment of harm to staff. Signal: repeated strike ballots.
rationale.SYS: Sector-wide funding pressures are real, but management's response prioritized cuts over structural staff protection. Signal: GBP140m cuts.
rationale.INT: Union alleges financially unjustified cuts ('we know there is no deficit'); stated values vs redundancy threat show consistency-under-pressure failure. Signal: 2025 strikes.

rationale.AWR: Post-2020 #payetonepfl, EPFL ran community-wide harassment and mental-health surveys and built a Trust and Support Network — structured proactive detection. Signal: Mental Health & Wellbeing Task Force survey.
rationale.EMP: Respect Compliance Office and TSN provide validation pathways for those harmed; created in direct response to student testimony. Signal: Respect Compliance Office.
rationale.ACT: Mental-health services and a reporting/case system exist; survey identified burnout/distress and targeted measures followed. Signal: mental-health survey-to-action.
rationale.EQU: Gender/diversity efforts active (Polyquity origins), but STEM-monoculture and doctoral precarity limit equity reach. Signal: diversity programs.
rationale.BND: Doctoral-student dependency on supervisors is a known risk; new harassment guidelines address some power asymmetry. Signal: harassment guidelines LEX 1.8.3.
rationale.ACC: Notable accountability: EPFL publicly acknowledged a harassment problem ('EPFL is acting on harassment'), appointed an RCO, and reported reforms. Signal: public harassment acknowledgment.
rationale.SYS: Task-force model addressed structural culture, not just incidents; some root-cause orientation. Signal: 6 working groups.
rationale.INT: Acted after student campaign but followed through with structural appointments and published procedures — reform appears genuine. Confidence: medium.

rationale.AWR: CAPS and Wolverine Wellness plus a settlement-mandated Coordinated Community Response Team improve detection, but Anderson abuse went undetected for 37 years. Signal: CCRT.
rationale.EMP: GEO protested ECRT's alleged mishandling of misconduct reports; survivors of Anderson abuse describe slow institutional empathy. Signal: GEO/ECRT protest.
rationale.ACT: Robust student services and the $490m Anderson settlement show capacity to respond, though responsiveness to live misconduct reports was contested. Signal: Anderson settlement.
rationale.EQU: Strong access mission (Go Blue Guarantee free tuition for in-state low-income), large aid programs lift equity. Signal: Go Blue Guarantee.
rationale.BND: 2023 GEO settlement (3-yr contract after long strike) improved grad-worker terms; gains came via strike, not initiative. Signal: GEO 2023 contract.
rationale.ACC: Anderson: $490m settlement and CCRT show reparative action, but only after decades and litigation; mixed self-disclosure. Signal: Anderson finalized settlement.
rationale.SYS: Settlement-funded structural prevention (CCRT) is genuine systemic learning; access advocacy strong. Signal: prevention infrastructure.
rationale.INT: Repeated misconduct scandals (Anderson, Stephenson, prior president misconduct) and refusal to bargain on Title IX show a persistent values-behavior gap. Signal: ECRT bargaining dispute.

rationale.AWR: CAPS and student services exist, but football hazing persisted undetected until an anonymous 2022 complaint — a serious detection failure. Signal: anonymous-email-triggered discovery.
rationale.EMP: Athletes alleging sexual hazing and race-based abuse describe institutional culture that failed to see them; felt care was low. Signal: hazing lawsuits.
rationale.ACT: University acted once aware: discontinued Camp Kenosha, added locker-room monitoring, education programs. Signal: post-investigation measures.
rationale.EQU: Strong financial-aid (no-loan) and access programs lift equity; athletics-culture failures were identity-tinged (race-based assaults alleged). Signal: aid program.
rationale.BND: Hazing represents a profound boundary/consent failure within athletics; reforms target oversight gaps. Signal: athletics misconduct-complaint gaps report.
rationale.ACC: Strong accountability response: fired Fitzgerald, commissioned independent Loretta Lynch review, published implementation plans, settled lawsuits. Signal: Lynch independent review.
rationale.SYS: Aimed to become a 'model for combating hazing' with systemic athletics-culture reforms — structural learning intent. Signal: accountability-and-culture implementation plan.
rationale.INT: Decisive firing carried cost (Fitzgerald's $130m suit, later settled), but the years-long failure beforehand and contested 'didn't know' framing temper integrity. Signal: Fitzgerald litigation.

rationale.AWR: Counseling centers exist per national mandate, but graduate-student distress and supervisor-pressure are documented sector problems; English-public detection data thin. LOW confidence. Signal: national counseling mandate.
rationale.EMP: Hierarchical advisor-student relationship and limited grievance independence constrain felt care; little public validation evidence. Signal: advisor dependency.
rationale.ACT: MOE-driven reforms (advisor-reassignment system, ban on non-academic tasks) provide some response capacity; effectiveness undocumented publicly. Signal: MOE reforms.
rationale.EQU: Strong national need-based aid and rural-quota access programs, offset by hukou/regional inequities; limited disaggregated reporting. Signal: national aid policy.
rationale.BND: Supervisor power over graduate students is a known boundary risk; 2021 staff stabbing of an administrator surfaced contract-precarity tensions. Signal: tenure/precarity tensions.
rationale.ACC: Limited public self-disclosure of institutional harm; accountability flows through state/MOE channels, not transparent self-reporting. Signal: state-channel accountability.
rationale.SYS: MOE structural reforms to advisor power show some root-cause response system-wide. Signal: advisor-reassignment reform.
rationale.INT: Thin English evidence; scored conservatively per PRD low-confidence cap (no composite >60). Confidence: low.

rationale.AWR: A 'Listening and Monitoring Unit' (cellule d'ecoute) covers violence/harassment/discrimination, plus Nightline Paris peer listening — structured detection across the federation. Signal: cellule d'ecoute.
rationale.EMP: Confidential listening units provide validation pathways, but the grande-ecole high-pressure culture limits felt care for doctoral/prep students. Signal: Nightline + unit.
rationale.ACT: Counseling and listening services exist; capacity across 29 doctoral schools is uneven and outcome data thin. Signal: doctoral college coordination.
rationale.EQU: French free/low-cost tuition and scholarship access aid equity, though grandes ecoles are socially selective (elite-reproduction critique). Signal: tuition access vs selectivity.
rationale.BND: Doctoral-school dispersion creates uneven labor/consent protections; federation structure complicates accountability. Signal: 29-school federation.
rationale.ACC: Anti-violence units signal acknowledgment of harassment risk; public self-disclosure of outcomes limited. Signal: violence/harassment unit.
rationale.SYS: Doctoral College centralizes some policy; structural critique of elite-reproduction is muted. Signal: doctoral policy coordination.
rationale.INT: Federated identity makes integrity hard to assess uniformly; scored conservatively. Confidence: medium.

rationale.AWR: CEDARS counseling and wellbeing services are well-resourced, but the university cannot detect or surface politically-sensitive distress in a surveillance environment. Signal: CEDARS vs NSL chilling effect.
rationale.EMP: Disbanding of the elected student union removed students' primary representative voice; mandatory NSL education replaced commemoration — felt care for dissenting students collapsed. Signal: student-union dissolution.
rationale.ACT: Material student services (counseling, housing, financial) are strong and responsive in non-political domains. Signal: CEDARS services.
rationale.EQU: Generous need-based aid and strong access for HK/mainland students; equity in identity/belief terms is constrained by NSL. Signal: financial aid.
rationale.BND: Academic-freedom restrictions and a proposed power to punish students for 'disrepute' chill expression — consent/autonomy boundaries narrowed. Signal: HRW report; disrepute proposal.
rationale.ACC: Midnight removal of the Pillar of Shame without due process, and suppression rather than acknowledgment of stakeholder harm, indicate low accountability. Signal: Pillar of Shame removal.
rationale.SYS: Institution aligned with NSL structures rather than critiquing forces harming its community; structural critique effectively foreclosed. Signal: mandatory NSL education.
rationale.INT: Stated academic-freedom values contradicted by self-censorship and HRW-documented decline; consistency under political pressure failed badly. Signal: HRW academic-freedom report.

rationale.AWR: Mandated counseling centers exist, but documented sector-wide graduate distress and supervisor-pressure suggest detection gaps; English-public data thin. LOW confidence. Signal: national counseling mandate.
rationale.EMP: Advisor-student hierarchy and limited independent grievance channels constrain felt care; little public validation evidence. Signal: advisor dependency.
rationale.ACT: MOE reforms (advisor-reassignment, non-academic-task bans) provide response scaffolding; effectiveness undocumented publicly. Signal: MOE reforms.
rationale.EQU: National need-based aid and rural-quota access aid equity; hukou/regional inequities and thin disaggregation. Signal: national aid policy.
rationale.BND: Supervisor power over graduate-student careers is a documented boundary risk across Chinese research universities. Signal: supervisor-exploitation reports.
rationale.ACC: Accountability runs through state/MOE rather than transparent institutional self-disclosure. Signal: state-channel accountability.
rationale.SYS: MOE structural reforms to advisor power apply system-wide; some root-cause orientation. Signal: advisor-reassignment reform.
rationale.INT: Thin English evidence; conservative scoring per low-confidence cap. Confidence: low.

rationale.AWR: Counseling and Wellness Services exist with a history of mental-health emphasis after past student-suicide clusters; high-cost urban stress is a known pressure. Signal: CWS history.
rationale.EMP: GSOC and newly-unionized contract faculty (89% vote) indicate workers sought voice they felt they lacked; felt care contested. Signal: CFU unionization.
rationale.ACT: Mental-health and emergency services are resourced; demand at a large urban campus strains responsiveness. Signal: CWS capacity.
rationale.EQU: High cost of attendance and historically modest Pell share weaken access equity, partly offset by recent aid expansion. Signal: cost-of-attendance vs Pell.
rationale.BND: GSOC contract sets meaningful grad minimums ($28-30/hr); contract-faculty job-security demands signal prior precarity now being addressed. Signal: GSOC + CFU contracts.
rationale.ACC: Standard Title IX structures; no recent system-wide self-disclosed scandal, but limited proactive transparency. Signal: Title IX office.
rationale.SYS: Global-network model and access initiatives, but affordability critique runs against its high-tuition business model. Signal: high-tuition model.
rationale.INT: Affordability and labor tensions (two unionization waves) suggest stated student-welfare values lag the cost/contingency reality. Signal: contract-faculty negotiations.

rationale.AWR: Counseling exists per mandate, but multiple student suicides reportedly tied to supervisor exploitation indicate serious detection/prevention failure. LOW confidence. Signal: suicide cases linked to supervisors.
rationale.EMP: Documented cases of supervisors putting students under extreme pressure with little recourse point to low felt care and weak validation. Signal: supervisor-exploitation reports.
rationale.ACT: MOE-mandated advisor-reassignment system and non-academic-task ban followed these cases, but institutional responsiveness was reactive and late. Signal: MOE response.
rationale.EQU: National need-based aid and access quotas aid equity; hukou/regional inequity and thin disaggregation remain. Signal: national aid policy.
rationale.BND: Supervisor power was directly implicated in student harm — a documented boundary/dependency failure. Signal: advisor-exploitation suicides.
rationale.ACC: Accountability came via MOE/state intervention rather than transparent institutional self-disclosure or reparative action. Signal: MOE intervention.
rationale.SYS: The cases prompted system-wide MOE structural reform of advisor power — root-cause response, externally driven. Signal: advisor-reassignment reform.
rationale.INT: Documented harm to students under supervisor pressure with reactive response weakens integrity; thin English evidence, scored conservatively. Confidence: low.

rationale.AWR: Counseling Center and Husky Health plus large UAW 4121 representation create multiple distress channels; capacity strained at ~50k enrollment. Signal: UAW 4121 + counseling.
rationale.EMP: UAW 4121 has pushed UW on Title IX protections, indicating workers felt the institution under-acknowledged misconduct concerns. Signal: UAW Title IX advocacy.
rationale.ACT: Student services functional; 2024 ASE strike resolved with rapid bargaining once leverage applied. Signal: 2024 ASE deal.
rationale.EQU: Strong access mission (Husky Promise free tuition for low-income WA residents) lifts equity substantially. Signal: Husky Promise.
rationale.BND: 2024 UAW deal delivered 36% base raises over three years — major improvement to grad-worker sustainability, won by strike. Signal: 2024 UAW contract.
rationale.ACC: Standard Title IX structures; union-flagged misconduct-handling concerns suggest accountability gaps not fully self-corrected. Signal: UAW Title IX updates.
rationale.SYS: Public-mission research university with sustained access advocacy and wellbeing investment. Signal: Husky Promise scale.
rationale.INT: Strong access values, but recurring strikes show worker-welfare commitments materialize mainly under pressure. Signal: 2024 strike.

rationale.AWR: Student counselling/wellbeing services exist, but UCU's 2025 formal trade dispute cites failures to protect staff welfare and inadequate complaints processes — detection gaps for staff harm. Signal: UCU welfare/safety dispute.
rationale.EMP: UCU alleges a 'democratic deficit' and that staff concerns on safeguarding go unaddressed; felt care for staff contested. Signal: UCU notification.
rationale.ACT: Student services functional; institutional responsiveness to staff complaints described as inadequate by the union. Signal: complaints-process critique.
rationale.EQU: Widening-participation programs and London access initiatives aid equity; casualisation and migrant-staff visa-fee burdens are equity weak points. Signal: casualisation/migrant-staff dispute.
rationale.BND: Fixed-term/casualised staff protections and migrant-staff support flagged as insufficient — boundary/sustainability gap. Signal: anti-casualisation dispute.
rationale.ACC: March 2025 formal dispute over complaints-process and academic-freedom failures suggests accountability shortfalls not yet resolved. Signal: 2025 trade dispute.
rationale.SYS: Some structural engagement (divestment/academic-freedom ballot), though it fell short of strike threshold. Signal: divestment ballot.
rationale.INT: UCU alleges 'glaring contradictions' in KCL's 'values-based impartiality' policy — a documented stated-values-vs-practice gap. Signal: values-impartiality critique.

rationale.AWR: Layered detection: University Counselling Centre, Residential Student Care Teams in every hall, Student Care Managers, and a Peer Helping Programme. Signal: RSCT + UCC structure.
rationale.EMP: Distributed care roles (faculty-in-residence, peer helpers) suggest relational support, though felt-experience evidence is limited. Signal: Peer Helping Programme.
rationale.ACT: Counselling and wellbeing services are resourced; harassment reports stated to be investigated thoroughly with graduated sanctions. Signal: UWO services.
rationale.EQU: National need-based aid and bursaries support access; equity framing universalist. Signal: national aid.
rationale.BND: Clear harassment-reporting and conduct pathways; a 2023 union-exco misconduct case was handled via counselling/discipline. Signal: misconduct-handling case.
rationale.ACC: Investigates misconduct with documented sanction ranges; less public aggregate transparency than NUS's half-yearly reports. Signal: conduct enforcement.
rationale.SYS: Strong wellbeing infrastructure but limited public structural advocacy in the Singapore context. Signal: wellbeing office.
rationale.INT: Comprehensive, institutionalized care structures suggest genuine (not purely performative) investment, though independent verification is thin. Signal: embedded care roles.

rationale.AWR: CAPS and UC systemwide wellbeing programs provide detection, but grad-worker testimony of 'untenable working conditions' shows distress was visible yet under-acted-on. Signal: 2022 grad protests.
rationale.EMP: Grad and post-grad students publicly described being unheard on cost-of-living; the OPHD Title IX office is structured but felt care contested. Signal: grad-worker accounts.
rationale.ACT: Student services functional; the core affordability need (stipends at ~30% of area median income) was addressed only via systemwide strike. Signal: stipend-vs-AMI gap.
rationale.EQU: Among the strongest US access profiles: very high Pell share and Hispanic-Serving Institution status lift equity substantially. Signal: high Pell / HSI status.
rationale.BND: 2022 UAW strike won improved pay/benefits, but pre-strike stipends classified as 'extremely low income' show a severe boundary/sustainability failure. Signal: UAW 2022 settlement.
rationale.ACC: Standard OPHD Title IX processes; limited public self-disclosure of systemic findings. Signal: OPHD.
rationale.SYS: Strong access mission and UC-systemwide advocacy on affordability and financial aid. Signal: UC access advocacy.
rationale.INT: Stated commitment to grad-worker welfare lagged badly behind sub-poverty stipends until forced by the largest US academic strike. Signal: 48k-worker UC strike.

rationale.AWR: Anti-discrimination/conflict officers, psychosocial services (via Studierendenwerk), and a Graduate Center consultation provide differentiated detection. Signal: conflict-counselling officers.
rationale.EMP: Dedicated contact points for students experiencing rights violations show validation pathways; felt-experience evidence thin in English. Signal: anti-discrimination advising.
rationale.ACT: Psychosocial support and counselling available; capacity/outcome data not public. Signal: psychosocial service.
rationale.EQU: German tuition-free access and a Women's Representative for equal-opportunity aid equity; disaggregated outcome data limited. Signal: Women's Representative.
rationale.BND: Formal anti-harassment policy exists; German fixed-term-contract precarity affects early-career researchers sector-wide. Signal: anti-harassment policy.
rationale.ACC: Established complaint/anti-discrimination structures; little public self-disclosed harm or reparative-action evidence. Signal: complaint structures.
rationale.SYS: Strong research-university planning; structural critique modest, evidence medium. Signal: governance.
rationale.INT: Comprehensive policy framework present; English evidence medium — scored conservatively. Confidence: medium.
